subject: Chemistry
q:
5g of Na2SO4 was dissolved in x g of H2O . The change in freezing point was found to be 3.82oC . If Na2SO4 is 81.5% ionised, the value of x

( Kf for water =1.86oC kg mol-1 ) is approximately:

(molar mass of S=32 g mol-1 and that of Na=23 g mol-1 )
question:
5g of Na2SO4 was dissolved in x g of H2O . The change in freezing point was found to be 3.82oC . If Na2SO4 is 81.5% ionised, the value of x

( Kf for water =1.86oC kg mol-1 ) is approximately:

(molar mass of S=32 g mol-1 and that of Na=23 g mol-1 )
solution:
Na2SO4→2Na++SO42-

i=1+3-1 0.815=2.63

3.82=1.86×2.63×5×1000142×x

∴ x=1.86×2.63×5000142×3.82

=45 gm

subject: Chemistry
q: Consider a binary solution of two volatile liquid components 1 and $2 . x_1$ and $y_1$ are the mole fractions of component 1 in liquid and vapour phase, respectively. The slope and intercept of the linear plot of $\frac{1}{x_1}$ vs $\frac{1}{y_1}$ are given respectively as:
question: Consider a binary solution of two volatile liquid components 1 and $2 . x_1$ and $y_1$ are the mole fractions of component 1 in liquid and vapour phase, respectively. The slope and intercept of the linear plot of $\frac{1}{x_1}$ vs $\frac{1}{y_1}$ are given respectively as:
solution:
Given mole fraction of liquid 1 in liquid and vapour phase is $x_1$ and $y_1$ respectively.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}_1=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}} y_1=\mathrm{P}_1^0 x_1 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}}{x_1}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_1^0}{y_1} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_2^0+x_1\left(\mathrm{P}_1^0+\mathrm{P}_2^0\right)}{x_1}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_1^0}{y_1} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_2^0}{x_1}+\left(\mathrm{P}_1^0+\mathrm{P}_2^0\right)=\frac{\mathrm{P}_1^0}{y_1} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_2^0}{x_1}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_1^0}{y_1}-\left(\mathrm{P}_1^0+\mathrm{P}_2^0\right) \\ & \frac{1}{x_1}=\frac{1}{y_2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{P}_1^0}{\mathrm{P}_2^0}\right)-\frac{\left(\mathrm{P}_1^0+\mathrm{P}_2^0\right)}{\mathrm{P}_2^0}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{x_1}=\frac{1}{y_2}\left(\frac{P_1^0}{P_2^0}\right)+\frac{\left(P_2^0-P_1^0\right)}{P_2^0} \\ & y=m x+c \\ & \text { Slope }=\frac{P_1^0}{P_2^0} ; \text { Intercept }=\frac{P_2^0-P_1^0}{P_2^0}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: For the solution of the gases <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> in water at <math><mn>298</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the Henry's law constants <math><mo>(</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow></msub><mo>)</mo></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.5,</mn><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>35</mn></math> and <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. The correct plot for the given data is:
question: For the solution of the gases <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> in water at <math><mn>298</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the Henry's law constants <math><mo>(</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow></msub><mo>)</mo></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.5,</mn><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>35</mn></math> and <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. The correct plot for the given data is:
solution:
Pgas=KH.Xgas

Where, Pgas is the partial pressure of gas.

KH is the Henry’s constant

Xgas is the mole fraction of gas

Pgas=KH1-XH2O

Pgas=KH-KHXH2O

On comparing this with the equation of straight line, y=mx+c, the intercept, c=KH. Thus, the gas having more value of Henry's law constant will have more value of intercept on the y-axis.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous solutions will exhibit the largest freezing point depression?
question: Which one of the following <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous solutions will exhibit the largest freezing point depression?
solution:
ΔTf=ikfm
As m is same for all electrolyte so
ΔTf α i m
Greater the value of i greater is depression in freezing point
For KHSO4i=3
And for Glucose, Ethylele Glycol & glycerol i=1

subject: Chemistry
q:
At 27°C, a solution containing 2.5 g of solute in 250.0 mL of solution exerts an osmotic pressure of 400 Pa. The molar mass of the solute is g mol-1 (Nearest integer)
(Given : R=0.083 L bar  -1mol-1 )
question:
At 27°C, a solution containing 2.5 g of solute in 250.0 mL of solution exerts an osmotic pressure of 400 Pa. The molar mass of the solute is g mol-1 (Nearest integer)
(Given : R=0.083 L bar  -1mol-1 )
solution:
The osmotic pressure of a non-electrolytic solution can be calculated as follows,

π=CRT

π = osmotic pressure

C = molarity

400 Pa105=2.5 gMo250/1000 L×0.83L-barK.mol×300 K

⇒ 400 × 10-5(1.01325) = 2.5 × 1000Mo × 250 0.083 × 300

⇒ 400 × 10-5 = 10Mo × 0.083 × 300

⇒ Mo = 10 × 0.83 × 300400 × 105

⇒ Mo = 10 × 0.083 × 34 × 105

⇒ Mo=62250 gram/mole

subject: Chemistry
q:
Molality of 0.8M H2SO4 solution (density 1.06 g cm-3 ) is _______×10-3 m.

Round off your answer to the nearest integer.
question:
Molality of 0.8M H2SO4 solution (density 1.06 g cm-3 ) is _______×10-3 m.

Round off your answer to the nearest integer.
solution:
1000 ml solution contains 0.8 mol of H2SO4

Mass of solution = 100cm3×1.06 gcm3= 1060 g

Mass of solute (H2SO4) =0.8 × 98 =78.4g

Mass of solvent = 1060 -78.4 =981.6 g

Molality = moles of H2SO4mass of solvent (in kg)

=0.8981.6×10−3 = 0.815m

= 815×10-3 m

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following statements regarding Henry's law is not correct?
question: Which one of the following statements regarding Henry's law is not correct?
options: Different gases have different <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>H</mi></mrow></msub></math> (Henry's law constant) values at the same temperature
The partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas in the solution
Higher the value of <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>H</mi></mrow></msub></math> at a given pressure, higher is the solubility of the gas in the liquids

The value of <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>H</mi></mrow></msub></math> increases with increase of temperature and <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>H</mi></mrow></msub></math> is function of the nature of the gas
solution:
p=KH.x

Where KH= Henry law constant

p = partial pressure

x = amount soluble

Henry's constant increases with increase in temperature while it decreases with increase in solubility.

Hence value of KH increases as solubility of gas increases is a wrong statement.

subject: Chemistry
q: Molality of an aqueous solution of urea is \(4.44 \mathrm{~m}\). Mole fraction of urea in solution is \(x \times 10^{-3}\). Value of \(x\) is \(\qquad\) - (Integer answer)
question: Molality of an aqueous solution of urea is \(4.44 \mathrm{~m}\). Mole fraction of urea in solution is \(x \times 10^{-3}\). Value of \(x\) is \(\qquad\) - (Integer answer)
solution:
Molality of urea is $4.44 \mathrm{~m}$, that means 4.44 moles of urea present in $1000 \mathrm{gm}$ of water.
$\therefore \mathrm{X}_{\text {ura }}=\frac{4.44}{4.44+\frac{1000}{18}}$
$=0.0740$
OR
$\begin{aligned} & 74 \times 10^{-3} \\ & X=74\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
40 g of glucose (Molar mass =180) is mixed with 200 mL of water. The freezing point of solution is __K. (Nearest integer)
[Given : Kf=1.86 K kg mol-1; Density of water =1.00 g cm-3; Freezing point of water =273.15 K
question:
40 g of glucose (Molar mass =180) is mixed with 200 mL of water. The freezing point of solution is __K. (Nearest integer)
[Given : Kf=1.86 K kg mol-1; Density of water =1.00 g cm-3; Freezing point of water =273.15 K
solution:
Molality =40180mol0.2Kg=109 molal

⇒ΔTf=Tf-Tf'=1.86×109

⇒Tf'=273.15-1.86×109

=271.08 K

≈271 K (nearest-integer)

q: Molecules of benzoic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> dimerise in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of benzene. ' <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi></math> ' <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of benzoic acid shows a depression in freezing point equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. If the percentage association of the acid to form dimer in the solution is <math><mn>80</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi></math> is: <math><mo>(</mo></math> Given that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Kmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, molar mass of benzoic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>122 </mn><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math>
question: Molecules of benzoic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> dimerise in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of benzene. ' <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi></math> ' <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of benzoic acid shows a depression in freezing point equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. If the percentage association of the acid to form dimer in the solution is <math><mn>80</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi></math> is: <math><mo>(</mo></math> Given that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Kmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, molar mass of benzoic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>122 </mn><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
ΔTf =kf×m×i …(i)

Molecular weight of C6H5COOH=112

n = 2  because benzoic acid is dimerised, β = 80 % = 0.8  

Here i=1n-1β+1

i=12-1 0.8+1

=0.6

Put the value in equation (i)

2=0.6×wt.of benzoic acid112301000×5

weight  of benzoic acid =2.4 g

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List I with List II.

List I List II
A. van't Hoff
factor, i		 I. Cryoscopic constant
B. kf II. Isotonic solutions
C. Solutions with same osmotic pressure III. Normal molar massAbnormal molar mas
D. Azeotropes IV. Solutions with same composition of vapour above it

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List I with List II.

List I List II
A. van't Hoff
factor, i		 I. Cryoscopic constant
B. kf II. Isotonic solutions
C. Solutions with same osmotic pressure III. Normal molar massAbnormal molar mas
D. Azeotropes IV. Solutions with same composition of vapour above it

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) van't Hoff factor, it is used in measuring colligative properties

i= Normal molar mass  Abnormal molar mass 

(B) kf= Cryoscopic constant or molal freezing constant

(C) Solutions with same osmotic pressure are known as isotonic solutions.

(D) Solutions with same composition of vapour over them are called Azeotrope or azeotrophic mixtures.

subject: Chemistry
q: A solution is prepared by adding 1 mole ethyl alcohol in 9 mole water. The mass percent of solute in the solution is ________ (Integer answer) (Given : Molar mass in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\) Ethyl alcohol : 46 water: 18)
question: A solution is prepared by adding 1 mole ethyl alcohol in 9 mole water. The mass percent of solute in the solution is ________ (Integer answer) (Given : Molar mass in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\) Ethyl alcohol : 46 water: 18)
solution:
Mass percent of Alcohol
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\text { Mass of ethyl alcohol }}{\text { Total mass of solution }} \times 100 \\ & =\frac{1 \times 46}{1 \times 46+9 \times 18} \times 100=\frac{4600}{208} \\ & =22.11 \text { Or } 22\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: Solution of 12 g of non-electrolyte (A) prepared by dissolving it in 1000 mL of water exerts the same osmotic pressure as that of 0.05 M glucose solution at the same temperature. The empirical formula of A is CH2O. The molecular mass of A is ________g. (Nearest integer)
question: Solution of 12 g of non-electrolyte (A) prepared by dissolving it in 1000 mL of water exerts the same osmotic pressure as that of 0.05 M glucose solution at the same temperature. The empirical formula of A is CH2O. The molecular mass of A is ________g. (Nearest integer)
solution:
The pressure which applied externally in the solution side to prevent the osmosis process is known as osmotic pressure. It can be denoted by π.

Since, (π1)non electrolyte = (π2)glucose
C1RT = C2RT
W1M1=W2M2

Empirical mass of 
12M1 = 0.05
M1 = 120.05 = 240

subject: Chemistry
q: For an ideal solution of two components <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>, which of the following is true?
question: For an ideal solution of two components <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>, which of the following is true?
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> interaction is stronger than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>. interactions

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> interactions are identical
solution: When two components <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> form an ideal solution, the interactions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><mo>–</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo> </mo><mo>–</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><mo>–</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are similar due to which no heat is absorbed or released and also volume after mixing remains same.

subject: Chemistry
q: A solution of sodium sulphate contains <math><mn>92</mn><mi> </mi><mi>g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Na</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ions per kilogram of water. The molality of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Na</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ions in that solution in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mol</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
question: A solution of sodium sulphate contains <math><mn>92</mn><mi> </mi><mi>g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Na</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ions per kilogram of water. The molality of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Na</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ions in that solution in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mol</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
solution:
Moles of Na+=Weight of Na+ ionsMolecular weight=9223=4
 

Molality is number of moles of solute present per Kg of Solvent

4 mole of Na+ are present in 1 kg of water Hence, molality of Na+ ions will be 4

subject: Chemistry
q: The vapour pressure of pure benzene and methyl benzene at \(27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) is given as 80 Torr and 24 Torr, respectively. The mole fraction of methyl benzene in vapour phase, in equilibrium with an equimolar mixture of those two liquids (ideal solution) at the same temperature is _______ \(\times 10^{-2}\) (nearest integer)
question: The vapour pressure of pure benzene and methyl benzene at \(27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) is given as 80 Torr and 24 Torr, respectively. The mole fraction of methyl benzene in vapour phase, in equilibrium with an equimolar mixture of those two liquids (ideal solution) at the same temperature is _______ \(\times 10^{-2}\) (nearest integer)
solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{X}_{\text {methylbenzene }} & =0.5 \\ \mathrm{Y}_{\text {methylbenzene }} & =\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\text {methylbenzene }}}{\mathrm{P}_{\text {total }}} \\ \mathrm{Y}_{\text {methylbenzene }} & =\frac{0.5 \times 24}{0.5 \times 80+0.5 \times 24} \\ & =\frac{12}{40+12}=0.23=23 \times 10^{-2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
If O2 gas is bubbled through water at 303 K, the number of millimoles of O2 gas that dissolve in 1 litre of water is____(Nearest integer) (Given : Henry's Law constant for O2 at 303 K is 46.82k bar and partial pressure of O2=0.920 bar)
(Assume solubility of O2 in water is too small, nearly negligible)
question:
If O2 gas is bubbled through water at 303 K, the number of millimoles of O2 gas that dissolve in 1 litre of water is____(Nearest integer) (Given : Henry's Law constant for O2 at 303 K is 46.82k bar and partial pressure of O2=0.920 bar)
(Assume solubility of O2 in water is too small, nearly negligible)
solution:
p=KH×x

0.920=46.82×103 bar ×mol of O2mol of H2O

0.920=46.82×103×mol of O2100018

0.920=46.82×18×nO2

0.92046.82×18=nO2

⇒1.09×10-3=n02

⇒m mol of O2=1 millimole

subject: Chemistry
q: The osmotic pressure exerted by a solution prepared by dissolving <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of protein of molar mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Pa</mi></math>. [integer value] (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>083</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> bar <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: The osmotic pressure exerted by a solution prepared by dissolving <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of protein of molar mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Pa</mi></math>. [integer value] (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>083</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> bar <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Osmotic pressure is the minimum pressure which needs to be applied to a solution to prevent the inward flow of its pure solvent across a semipermeable membrane.It is a colligative property.

π=CRTWhere,π=Osmotic pressureC=MolarityT=Temperature.

π=CRT=2×10360×103×200×8.31×300 × 1000                                =415Pa

subject: Chemistry
q: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>363</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, the vapour pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kPa</mi></math> and that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mi>kPa</mi></math>. One mole of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are mixed. Assuming that this solution is ideal, the vapour pressure of the mixture is___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kPa</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>Round of to the Nearest Integer<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>)</mo></math>.
question: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>363</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, the vapour pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kPa</mi></math> and that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mi>kPa</mi></math>. One mole of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are mixed. Assuming that this solution is ideal, the vapour pressure of the mixture is___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kPa</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>Round of to the Nearest Integer<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>)</mo></math>.
solution:
Given PA0=21 kPa  ⇒PB0=18 kPa

→ An Ideal solution is prepared by mixing 1 mole A and 2 mole B.

→XA=13 and XB=23

→ According to Raoult's law

PT=XAPA0+XBPB0

⇒  PT=13×21+23×18

⇒  PT=7+12=19 kPa

subject: Chemistry
q:
The freezing point of benzene decreases by 0.45°C on adding 0.2 g of acetic acid to 20 g of benzene. If acetic acid associates to form a dimer in benzene, then what is the percentage association of acetic acid in benzene?

Kf for benzene=5.12 K kg mol-1
question:
The freezing point of benzene decreases by 0.45°C on adding 0.2 g of acetic acid to 20 g of benzene. If acetic acid associates to form a dimer in benzene, then what is the percentage association of acetic acid in benzene?

Kf for benzene=5.12 K kg mol-1
solution:
2CH3COOH⇌CH3COOH21-αα2

i=1-α2

ΔTf=i×Kf×m

0.45=i×5.12×0.2×100060×20

i=0.527=1-α2 

⇒α=0.946 or 94.6%

subject: Chemistry
q:
Considering acetic acid dissociates in water, its dissociation constant is \(6.25 \times 10^{-5}\). If \(5 \mathrm{~mL}\) of acetic acid is dissolved in 1 litre water, the solution will freeze at \(-x \times 10^{-2}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\), provided pure water freezes at \(0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\).
\(x=\) ______ . (Nearest integer)
Given :
\(\begin{aligned}
& \left(\mathrm{K}_f\right)_{\text {water }}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \text {. } \\
& \text { density of acetic acid is } 1.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \text {. } \\
& \text { molar mass of water }=18 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \text {. } \\
& \text { molar mass of acetic acid=60 } \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \text {. } \\
& \text { density of water }=1 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}
\end{aligned}\)
Acetic acid dissociates as \(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{\ominus}+\mathrm{H}^{\oplus}\)
question:
Considering acetic acid dissociates in water, its dissociation constant is \(6.25 \times 10^{-5}\). If \(5 \mathrm{~mL}\) of acetic acid is dissolved in 1 litre water, the solution will freeze at \(-x \times 10^{-2}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\), provided pure water freezes at \(0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\).
\(x=\) ______ . (Nearest integer)
Given :
\(\begin{aligned}
& \left(\mathrm{K}_f\right)_{\text {water }}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \text {. } \\
& \text { density of acetic acid is } 1.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \text {. } \\
& \text { molar mass of water }=18 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \text {. } \\
& \text { molar mass of acetic acid=60 } \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \text {. } \\
& \text { density of water }=1 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}
\end{aligned}\)
Acetic acid dissociates as \(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{\ominus}+\mathrm{H}^{\oplus}\)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Mass of } \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}=\mathrm{V} \times \mathrm{d} \\ & =5 \mathrm{ml} \times 1.2 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{ml} \\ & =6 \mathrm{gm} \\ & \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}}=\frac{6}{60}=0.1 \mathrm{~mol} \\ & \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}} \approx \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}}=\frac{0.1}{1}=0.1 \mathrm{M} \\ & \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}+\mathrm{H}^{+}\end{aligned}$
C
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C} \alpha \quad \mathrm{C} \alpha \quad \mathrm{C} \alpha \\ & \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{C} \alpha^2}{1-\alpha} \\ & 1-\alpha \approx 1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{C} \alpha^2 \\ & \alpha=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{Ka}}{\mathrm{C}}}=\sqrt{\frac{6.25 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1}}=25 \times 10^{-3}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { V.f. }(\mathrm{i})=1+\alpha(\mathrm{n}-1)=1+\alpha(2-1)=1+\alpha \\ & =1+25 \times 10^{-3}=1.025 \\ & \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{m} \\ & =(1.025)(1.86)(0.1) \\ & =0.19 \\ & =19 \times 10^{-2}\end{aligned}$

q: Elevation in boiling point for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> molal solution of glucose in water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The depression in freezing point for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> molal solution of glucose in water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The ratio of molal elevation constant to molal depression constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub><mo>/</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: Elevation in boiling point for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> molal solution of glucose in water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The depression in freezing point for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> molal solution of glucose in water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The ratio of molal elevation constant to molal depression constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub><mo>/</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Elevation in boiling point ∆Tb=ikbm1

Depression in freezing point ∆Tf=ikfm2

4=ikb1.5

4=ikf4.5

Dividing both:

kbkf=31

Hence, The ratio of molal elevation constant to molal depression constant is  3:1

subject: Chemistry
q:
What is the mole fraction of water in $10$% by weight (w/w) of aqueous urea solution?
[Given: Molar mass of H, O, C and N are $1$, $16$, $12$ and $14$ g mol$^{-1}$ respectively.]
question:
What is the mole fraction of water in $10$% by weight (w/w) of aqueous urea solution?
[Given: Molar mass of H, O, C and N are $1$, $16$, $12$ and $14$ g mol$^{-1}$ respectively.]
solution:
$10\%$ w/w aqueous urea solution means $10$ g of urea is present in $100$ g of solution.

Mass of urea $= 10$ g
Mass of water $= 100 - 10 = 90$ g

Molar mass of urea ($\text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2$) $= 14 + 2 + 12 + 16 + 14 + 2 = 60$ g mol$^{-1}$
Molar mass of water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) $= 18$ g mol$^{-1}$

Moles of urea, $n_{\text{urea}} = \dfrac{10}{60} = \dfrac{1}{6}$ mol
Moles of water, $n_{\text{water}} = \dfrac{90}{18} = 5$ mol

Total moles $= 5 + \dfrac{1}{6} = \dfrac{31}{6}$ mol

Mole fraction of water, $x_{\text{water}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{water}}}{n_{\text{water}} + n_{\text{urea}}} = \dfrac{5}{\dfrac{31}{6}} = \dfrac{30}{31} \approx 0.967$

Answer: $0.967$

subject: Chemistry
q: Choose the correct statement with respect to the vapour pressure of a liquid among the following :
question: Choose the correct statement with respect to the vapour pressure of a liquid among the following :
solution:
The pressure exerted by the vapors over the liquid surface when liquid and vapour are in equilibrium is called vapour pressure. Vapour pressure increases with an increase with temperature.
Average kinetic energy of the gas molecules is directly proportional to the absolute temperature. At higher temperatures, more molecules from the liquid have enough KE to escape from the surface of the liquid. Which will increase vapor pressure.

For phase equilibrium for liquid & vapour phases

dP dT = Δ S Δ V = Δ H vap T . Δ V vap

dP dT = Δ H vap T RT P = Δ H vap × P RT 2 for 1 mole

dP P = Δ H vap RT 2 dT ⇒

The relation between temperature and vapor pressure is given by the Clausius-Clapeyron equation.

ln P 2 P 1 = Δ H vap R T 2 - T 1 T 1 T 2

undefined

ie. vapour increases exponentially with rise in temp ie.
Non linear increase.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The osmotic pressure of a living cell is 12 atm at 300 K. The strength of sodium chloride solution that is isotonic with the living cell at this temperature is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{L}^{-1}$. (Nearest integer)
Given : $\mathrm{R}=0.08 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Assume complete dissociation of NaCl
(Given : Molar mass of Na and Cl are 23 and $35.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.)
question:
The osmotic pressure of a living cell is 12 atm at 300 K. The strength of sodium chloride solution that is isotonic with the living cell at this temperature is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{L}^{-1}$. (Nearest integer)
Given : $\mathrm{R}=0.08 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Assume complete dissociation of NaCl
(Given : Molar mass of Na and Cl are 23 and $35.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.)
solution:
For osmotic pressure, using the van 't Hoff equation: $\pi = iMRT$.
The living cell has osmotic pressure of 12 atm.
For an isotonic NaCl solution, the osmotic pressures must be equal.
For NaCl solution: $\pi = i \times M \times R \times T$, where $i = 2$ (complete dissociation into Na⁺ and Cl⁻).
$12 = 2 \times M \times 0.08 \times 300$
$12 = 48M$
$M = 0.25$ mol/L
Molar mass of NaCl = 23 + 35.5 = 58.5 g/mol.
Concentration in g/L = $0.25 \times 58.5 = 14.625 \approx 15$ g/L

q:
CO2 gas is bubbled through water during a soft drink manufacturing process at 298 K. If CO2 exerts a partial pressure of 0.835 bar then x m mol of CO2 would dissolve in 0.9 L of water. The value of x is _______. (Nearest integer)

(Henry's law constant for CO2 at 298 K is 1.67×103 bar)
question:
CO2 gas is bubbled through water during a soft drink manufacturing process at 298 K. If CO2 exerts a partial pressure of 0.835 bar then x m mol of CO2 would dissolve in 0.9 L of water. The value of x is _______. (Nearest integer)

(Henry's law constant for CO2 at 298 K is 1.67×103 bar)
solution:
From Henry's law

Pgas=KH·Xgas

0.835=1.67×103×nCO20.9×100018

nCO2=0.025

Millimoles of CO2=0.025×1000=25
